g.data-narration: Welcome to Exception Priorities in ARM Cortex-M Microcontrollers. This topic covers how the NVIC handles multiple interrupts efficiently.
An exception disrupts normal program flow. In ARM Cortex-M, this includes hardware interrupts, faults, system calls like SVCall, and reset events.
g.text: Any event that disrupts normal program execution flow
System Exceptions: Reset, NMI, Hard Fault, SVCall, PendSV, SysTick

g.data-narration: When multiple exceptions occur simultaneously, priorities determine which gets serviced first. Critical tasks like motor control need immediate attention.
g.text: When multiple exceptions occur at once, the processor needs a rule to decide
which exception should be handled first!

g.data-narration: ARM Cortex-M supports various exception types with fixed default priorities. Reset has highest priority (-3), followed by NMI (-2), then Hard Fault (-1).
g.text: Usage Fault, Bus Fault, MemManage Fault

g.data-narration: When an exception occurs: 1) Detect exception, 2) Save processor state to stack, 3) Load handler from vector table, 4) Execute handler, 5) Restore state.
g.text: Stack Frame: 8 registers (R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) = 32 bytes

g.data-narration: Lower numeric priority value means HIGHER importance! Priority 0 is highest (most critical), while Priority 255 is lowest. This confuses many students.
NVIC uses IP registers (IP[0]-IP[239]) to store priority for each interrupt. Each interrupt has 8-bit priority field, but implementations use fewer bits.
Priority grouping splits priority bits into group priority (for preemption) and sub-priority (for ordering). PRIGROUP field in AIRCR controls this split.
Higher-priority exception (lower number) can preempt lower-priority handler. The NVIC handles nesting automatically - no software needed for basic cases.
g.text: Rule: Exception A preempts Exception B if Priority(A) < Priority(B)

g.data-narration: Three masking registers control exception handling: PRIMASK masks all except NMI/HardFault, BASEPRI masks below threshold, FAULTMASK also masks HardFault.
In this example, Timer0 (IRQ 19) gets priority 2 and UART0 (IRQ 21) gets priority 5. Timer0 can preempt UART0 since 2 < 5.
BASEPRI allows critical sections to run without interruption from lower-priority interrupts. Set BASEPRI to mask priority 3 and below.
g.text: Example 2: Using BASEPRI for Critical Sections

g.data-narration: Each exception saves 8 registers (32 bytes) to stack. Nested exceptions require more stack space. Design for worst-case nesting depth.
g.text: (R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR)
Each nesting level adds 32 bytes - allocate for worst-case!

g.data-narration: Key formulas: Max priority = 2^n - 1, nesting possible when Priority(A) < Priority(B). Remember: lower number = higher priority!
g.text: Remember: Lower Priority Number = Higher Importance!
